expect: r < -0.4 between domain refractive index and ISO atlas appearances. High-density domains appear less in cross-domain atlas (total internal reflection).
actual: FALSIFIED. Spearman r = +0.70, Pearson r = +0.87 (n=5 matched domains). Correlation is strongly POSITIVE — dense domains appear MORE in ISO atlas, not less. Data: meta/swarm (n=1.79, ISO=23), thermodynamics/physics (n=1.44, ISO=12), brain/neuroscience (n=1.21, ISO=10), economy/economics (n=1.06, ISO=15), governance (n=0.79, ISO=2).
diff: Predicted r < -0.4, got r = +0.70. Direction completely wrong. The 'total internal reflection' metaphor breaks down: dense domains are not trapped — they are hubs. Internally rich domains are also cross-domain productive. Limitation: only 5 of 21 refractive-index domains matched ISO atlas hub names (naming mismatch between internal domain names and ISO atlas entries).
limitations: Small n=5 due to naming mismatch between refractive-index domains and ISO atlas domains. 16/21 domains could not be matched. Result direction is clear but magnitude uncertain.
prescriptions: Retire the 'total internal reflection' metaphor — domain density predicts cross-domain influence, not isolation
Standardize domain naming between tools to enable broader correlation analysis
